Problem: Measuring playful activities and their relationship with physical activity in public open spaces is difficult; few studies use movement trajectories to measure play.
Research Gap: Few studies have been conducted to measure play because it is not easy to distinguish from other behaviours; existing studies do not fully leverage trajectories to classify playful movement and link it to site features and physical activity.
Methodology: Computer vision to generate pedestrian trajectories from video data; trajectory classification using indicators (detour ratio, bounding box area, long-to-short edge ratio, length, duration); multinomial logistic regression to relate trajectory types to interaction with site features; physical activity estimation (MET-minutes) based on movement speed.
Key Results: Four trajectory types identified (Playful, Strolling, Sporty, Passive); interaction with specific site features (heart-shaped seating, bench, pavilion) significantly associated with different trajectory types compared to the passive group; different trajectory types exhibited significant differences in the amount and intensity of physical activity.

Problem: Vision-based monitoring of social distancing and critical density for COVID-19; Need for robust people detection in crowded, real-world scenarios with occlusions.
Research Gap: How to define appropriate averaging weights and coefficients for long-term crowd risk maps; Need for further study on normalizing maps over time.
Methodology: Proposed a YOLO-based object detection model fine-tuned on human categories from Google Open Images; Algorithm for tracking, red-zone prediction, and generating 2D/3D crowd risk maps; Evaluated on the Oxford Town Centre (OTC) dataset.
Key Results: System successfully identified crowded zones with social distancing breaches; Demonstrated feasibility of monitoring and risk assessment with high confidence and accuracy; Model performance compared against state-of-the-art methods using Precision, Recall, and FPS metrics.

Problem: Achieving high speed and accuracy in generic object detection.
Research Gap: Improving the speed and accuracy of existing object detection frameworks.
Methodology: Incremental improvements to the YOLO architecture (YOLOv2, YOLOv3, YOLOv4); Unified, real-time detection framework.
Key Results: Series of models (YOLO, YOLOv2, YOLOv3, YOLOv4) that are better, faster, and stronger than previous iterations.

Problem: Accurate object detection and semantic segmentation.
Research Gap: Improving feature extraction for detection and segmentation tasks.
Methodology: Region-based Convolutional Neural Network (R-CNN); Rich feature hierarchies.

Problem: Speeding up the region-based object detection process.
Research Gap: Reducing the computational bottleneck of multi-stage pipelines.
Methodology: Fast R-CNN; Single-stage training that jointly learns to classify object proposals and refine their spatial locations.
Key Results: Improved speed and accuracy over R-CNN.

Problem: Achieving real-time object detection with region proposals.
Research Gap: Eliminating the computational cost of external region proposal methods.
Methodology: Faster R-CNN; Introduces a Region Proposal Network (RPN) that shares full-image convolutional features with the detection network.
Key Results: Enables real-time object detection with a unified, neural network-based system.

Research Gap: Balancing speed and accuracy in a single forward pass of the network.
Methodology: SSD: Single Shot MultiBox Detector; Uses a single deep neural network to predict bounding boxes and object categories.
Key Results: High accuracy and superior speed compared to methods that require object proposals.

Problem: Handling class imbalance in dense object detection.
Research Gap: Addressing the foreground-background class imbalance problem in one-stage detectors.
Methodology: RetinaNet; Introduces Focal Loss to focus training on hard examples.
Key Results: Achieves accuracy of state-of-the-art two-stage detectors while maintaining high speed.

Problem: Automating the monitoring of social distancing in public spaces via real-time human detection and tracking.
Research Gap: The need for an efficient, real-time framework that balances detection accuracy with computational speed for practical deployment; Addressing privacy concerns and false positives in a public monitoring system.
Methodology: A deep learning framework using fine-tuned object detection models (Faster RCNN, SSD, YOLO v3) on a custom dataset from Open Images and Oxford Town Center footage; A tracking module using a combination of Mahalanobis distance and cosine appearance metrics; A novel "violation index" (ratio of people to social groups) to quantify distancing breaches.
Key Results: Inception v2 was identified as the most efficient backbone for Faster RCNN/SSD due to its high accuracy-to-parameters ratio; The fine-tuned models were used to detect and track individuals, forming social groups based on a 3D feature space (x, y, depth); The framework successfully visualized violations in real-time with color-coded groups and a statistical violation index.

Problem: Quantifying vitality in small public spaces is challenging.
Research Gap: Existing methods for large-scale spaces have low data accuracy and are not systematic or complete for small public spaces.
Methodology: A computer vision-based framework to extract human trajectories from videos and calculate quantitative indicators (number of people, duration of stay, motion speed, trajectory diversity, trajectory complexity); Stepwise multiple linear regression to model the relationship between indicators and expert-rated overall vitality.
Key Results: A significant multiple linear regression model was constructed (N=48, R²=.781, Sig. = .000); Number of people, duration of stay, trajectory diversity, and trajectory complexity were significant predictors of overall vitality (Sig. < 0.05); Motion speed was not a significant predictor in the final model.
